instructions: You are a thoughtful, patient, and articulate assistant. When answering, first confirm the core of the question, then provide a clear and well-structured explanation. Use steps or bullet points where helpful. Be precise — do not exaggerate or fabricate.

content.text:
You want a short, shareable summary of the recommendations we discussed. Below is a concise, actionable summary your team can use.

Purpose and high-level principles
- Know your audience: tailor docs for beginners, integrators, or reference users — not all at once.
- Lead with the outcome: start each section with what the reader will be able to do.
- Separate tutorial from reference: tutorials show step-by-step flows; reference lists endpoints/fields succinctly.

What to add on top of auto-generated OpenAPI docs
- One-line purpose for each endpoint (what it actually does).
- A concrete example request + response (realistic payloads).
- Common pitfalls / gotchas (pagination type, limits, timezone, retention windows).
- Quickstart or 5-minute guide that chains 3–4 calls to accomplish a real task.
- Group endpoints by user task (e.g., “Onboarding a new team member”) rather than only by resource.

Suggested endpoint documentation structure (compact template)
- Title / one-line summary
- Quick example (curl or code)
- Parameters & filtering notes (what values are accepted, default behavior)
- Pagination behavior (cursor vs offset, how to request next page)
- Common pitfalls / edge cases
- Errors and expected error codes (and what to do)
- Owner / who to contact

Example (how GET /orders should read)
- One-line: “List orders filtered by status and date range.”
- Quick example: curl showing query params and auth.
- Pagination: “cursor-based; response includes next_cursor and has_more.”
- Filtering notes: allowed status values, date semantics.
- Common pitfalls: e.g., limit cap, retention window, timezone handling.

Keeping docs up-to-date (practical, high-impact steps)
- Co-locate docs with code: docs/ in the API repo so PRs can update docs alongside code.
- CI enforcement: fail PRs when OpenAPI/schema changes but corresponding doc files are not updated.
- Run doc tests: execute example requests from the docs against a test environment in CI to catch broken examples.
- Version docs: publish docs per API version (keep last N versions live).
- Assign an owner: rotate a “docs sheriff” to review doc PRs each sprint.

Documenting internal-only endpoints
- Keep minimal but essential: one-line description, auth requirements, request/response schema, error codes, and owner/contact.
- Aim for ~15 lines per internal endpoint — enough to troubleshoot in an incident.

Quick checklist to get started (first 2–4 actions)
- Pick 1–2 high-traffic endpoints and rewrite their docs using the template above.
- Add runnable examples and include them in CI tests.
- Add a PR check that flags schema-vs-doc mismatches.
- Add an owner field to each endpoint doc and announce the rotation for the docs owner role.

Offer to help
- I can rewrite one of your real endpoints into this format, or draft a CI script to run examples from your docs — tell me which you’d prefer.

Would you like a one-page markdown file of this summary that you can drop into your repo?
